data.title: Electric & Fusion
data.article:
In 1969, Miles Davis walked into the studio with electric instruments and changed jazz forever.

*In a Silent Way* (1969) was the soft opening: meditative drones and electric piano lapping against each other like a tide. *Bitches Brew* (1970) was the explosion — a double album of studio-assembled sonic collage that invented jazz fusion and launched the careers of Chick Corea, John McLaughlin, Wayne Shorter, and Herbie Hancock.

The electric years were relentless. *Jack Johnson* (1971) channelled Sly Stone and James Brown into a two-track funk juggernaut. *On the Corner* (1972) went further, incorporating Karlheinz Stockhausen's ideas about rhythm loops into a street-level groove that baffled critics and enthralled a new generation.

Miles toured continuously with bands that grew louder, heavier, and more polyrhythmic. Then in 1975, exhausted and in pain from a deteriorating hip, he stopped entirely.

He would not return to music until 1981.
